title: 507 Hectares, 12 Fabs: Inside TSMC's Largest Taiwan Expansion Yet
gist: TSMC is quietly developing a 507-hectare, up-to-12-fab campus in southern Taiwan (Shalun) potentially dedicated to 1.4nm and 1nm production, a project larger than its Arizona site whose progress will hinge on environmental review and ecological-preservation battles.

themes.theme: Shalun megasite scale and process node ambitions

insights.claim: TSMC is quietly preparing a 507-hectare campus in southern Taiwan with plans for as many as 12 fabs, which could become the largest fab expansion project in the company's history.
insights.speaker: J
insights.so_what: A 12-fab, 507-hectare buildout signals TSMC is committing the bulk of its next-decade leading-edge capacity to Taiwan rather than diversifying further offshore, relevant for supply-chain concentration risk assessments.
insights.quote: In southern Taiwan, TSMC is quietly preparing what could become the largest fab expansion project in its history: a 507-hectare campus with plans for as many as 12 fabs

insights.claim: The Shalun campus fabs are potentially dedicated to 1.4nm and even 1nm production, indicating TSMC is earmarking its most advanced future nodes for this site.
insights.speaker: J
insights.so_what: If confirmed, this ties TSMC's bleeding-edge node roadmap (beyond current 2nm/1.4nm plans) directly to a single Taiwan mega-campus, making that site a chokepoint for future leading-edge supply.
insights.quote: potentially dedicated to 1.4nm and even 1nm production

insights.claim: The Shalun project is larger than TSMC's Arizona site and could define TSMC's Taiwan footprint for the next decade.
insights.speaker: J
insights.so_what: Investors tracking TSMC's geographic capacity split (Taiwan vs. Arizona vs. Japan) should treat Shalun as the largest single data point yet that Taiwan remains the anchor for leading-edge capacity despite overseas expansion.
insights.quote: The project is larger than TSMC's Arizona site and could define the company's Taiwan footprint for the next decade.

insights.claim: Building the fabs themselves is described as only half the challenge; the harder part is navigating environmental reviews, ecological preservation, and competing societal priorities around balancing semiconductor leadership with sustainability.
insights.speaker: J
insights.so_what: Watch Taiwan's environmental review process and ecological-preservation disputes as a leading indicator for potential delays to TSMC's Shalun timeline and, by extension, future leading-edge capacity ramp.
insights.quote: Yet building fabs is only half the story. The real challenge lies in navigating environmental reviews, ecological preservation, and the competing priorities of a society trying to balance semiconductor leadership with sustainability.

insights.claim: The outcome of the political and ecological battle over the Shalun site may reveal how far Taiwan's government and society are willing to go to keep TSMC's future manufacturing anchored domestically rather than shifting further offshore.
insights.speaker: J
insights.so_what: A test case for Taiwan's willingness to prioritize semiconductor leadership over local environmental and community objections, with implications for how much future TSMC capacity stays onshore versus moving abroad.
insights.quote: The battle over Shalun may ultimately reveal how far Taiwan is willing to go to keep TSMC's future anchored at home.
